source.title: Fatal Vessel Leak, Vapor Cloud Explosion, and Fire at Yenkin-Majestic Resin Plant

event.top_event: Loss of Containment - Vapor Cloud Explosion and Fire
event.incident_type: Vessel Leak, Vapor Cloud Explosion, and Fire
actions_taken: Employee A attempted to turn off agitator but could not reach emergency stop button due to vapor obscuring visibility
Employee A evacuated the kettle room approximately 80 seconds after release began
Employee B assisted Employee A in evacuation
More than 100 firefighters responded including hazmat teams
Fire was extinguished after approximately 11 hours
Firefighters rescued two injured employees trapped in collapsed building
Ohio EPA responded and contained firefighting water runoff entering Alum Creek
Yenkin-Majestic demolished the severely damaged resin plant over 19-month period
event.summary: On April 8, 2021, at approximately 12:02 a.m., a mixture of flammable naphtha solvent vapors and resin liquid became pressurized and released through the seal of a closed manway on Kettle 3 at the Yenkin-Majestic OPC Polymers resin plant in Columbus, Ohio. The release occurred after the kettle operator turned on the agitator, which caused rapid vaporization of solvent that had been added to the hot resin while the agitator was unexpectedly off. The flammable vapor cloud spread throughout the enclosed building and outside. Approximately two minutes later, at 12:04 a.m., the vapor cloud ignited, causing an explosion and large fire. One employee was fatally injured from thermal injuries and inhalation of combustion products. Eight other employees were transported to hospitals with injuries including third-degree burns and limb fractures, with one requiring leg amputation. Property damage exceeded $90 million.
recommendations: Update mechanical integrity procedures for all process vessels in highly hazardous chemicals service, including pressure vessels not exceeding 15 psig, to adopt alteration guidance in API 510 or National Board Inspection Code Part 3

Assess and document applicable design, construction, and alteration standards for all pressure vessels in highly hazardous chemicals service, adopting at minimum PIP VESLP001 Low-Pressure Welded Vessel Specification
Implement program to assess pressure vessels against updated RAGAGEP at least once every five years
Develop and implement written procedures to document and maintain records of all inspections, alterations, and maintenance/repairs on all pressure vessels in highly hazardous chemicals service
Demonstrate use of prevention through design using hierarchy of controls in future resin plant designs, prioritizing inherently safer design and engineering controls
Identify and document all equipment that could release flammable materials and install LEL detectors in accordance with CCPS guidelines
Ensure detection of hazardous conditions automatically triggers both visual and audible alarms to alert plant personnel
Develop and implement employee training on actions to take when alarms are activated, such as prompt evacuation
Develop and implement requirements for personnel to wear flame-resistant uniforms in all operating areas that process flammable chemicals
Update employee training material to include requirement for and purpose of PPE use
API to develop specific design, construction, and alteration guidance for low-pressure process vessels in flammable and other highly hazardous chemicals service not exceeding 15 psig
ASME to assist API in developing design, construction, and alteration guidance for low-pressure vessels in flammable and other highly hazardous chemicals service not exceeding 15 psig
key_phrases: pressure vessel not designed or pressure tested to appropriate design pressure
lack of audible alarms for flammable gas detection

hazards.name: Rapid pressure increase in reactor vessel

threats.name: Agitator shutdown while solvent added to hot resin
Manway seal failure due to inadequate design pressure
Rapid vaporization of solvent upon agitator restart
Rupture disk failure to relieve pressure
Ignition source contact with flammable vapor cloud

controls.name: Mechanical integrity procedures for pressure vessels
controls.barrier_role: Ensure vessels are designed, constructed, and tested to appropriate design pressure

supporting_text: Yenkin-Majestic did not adequately ensure the mechanical integrity of a new 20-inch manway that was installed on a process vessel (Kettle 3) approximately three months before the incident
After the new manway was added, Yenkin-Majestic performed a leak check up to 4 psig and allowed Kettle 3 to continue operating for approximately three months until the incident
On the day of the incident, Kettle 3's new manway did not withstand more than 9 psig of pressure during a process upset and leaked hot resin and flammable solvent vapor into the facility
Yenkin-Majestic did not follow basic quality assurance practices, such as adequately pressure testing equipment after alterations

controls.name: Agitator status indication and alarm system
controls.barrier_role: Alert operator to agitator status to prevent solvent addition when agitator is off

supporting_text: Yenkin-Majestic had not installed or configured interlocks to prevent solvent addition to the kettle when the agitator was off during normal batch operations and relied upon computer panel status indicators, that were not equipped with alarms, to communicate the agitator status to the operator

At 10:22 p.m. the Kettle 3 agitator unexpectedly shut down. Employee A returned a few minutes later, at 10:25 p.m., and did not realize that the agitator had shut down while he was away; no alarms were active at the time to indicate a process abnormality, either

Around midnight, about 90 minutes after Employee A pushed the Batch Done button, the kettle temperature was recorded as 424 °F—approximately 100 °F higher than he expected after 90 minutes of cooling. At this time, Employee A began to investigate potential causes for this process abnormality. He looked into the kettle through the glass window of the manway and noticed for the first time that the agitator was off

controls.name: Interlocks to prevent solvent addition when agitator is off
controls.barrier_role: Prevent hazardous process condition by blocking solvent addition during agitator shutdown
controls.lod_basis: Hierarchy of controls - engineering control preferred over administrative

supporting_text: Yenkin-Majestic had not installed or configured interlocks to prevent solvent addition to the kettle when the agitator was off during normal batch operations
The system design allowed an operator to add solvent to the hot-resin-filled kettle while, unknown to the operator, the agitator (which was supposed to mix the liquids in the kettle) was off

controls.name: High-pressure alarm at 2 psig
controls.barrier_role: Alert operator to pressure increase for manual intervention

supporting_text: The kettle was configured with a high-pressure alarm at 2 pounds per square inch gauge (psig)
During the April 8, 2021, incident, the high-pressure alarm and trip activated after Kettle 3's pressure began to rise

controls.name: Automatic high-pressure trip at 4 psig with emergency cooling interlock
controls.barrier_role: Automatically shut down furnace and activate cooling water to reduce pressure

supporting_text: The kettle was configured with an automatic high-pressure trip at 4 psig. The high-pressure trip activated the emergency cooling safety interlock, which was designed to shut down the furnace and turn on cooling water to the coils to aid in reducing the temperature and pressure inside the kettle
During the April 8, 2021, incident, the high-pressure alarm and trip activated after Kettle 3's pressure began to rise, and the rupture disk did not burst

controls.name: Rupture disk pressure relief system
controls.barrier_role: Relieve excessive pressure by opening at design burst pressure to prevent vessel rupture
controls.lod_basis: Pressure relief device for overpressure protection

supporting_text: The Kettle 3 emergency pressure-relief system included one 8-inch rupture disk, used to protect the vessel from high-pressure conditions, which Yenkin-Majestic documented as having a burst pressure between 13.5 and 15 psig
During the April 8, 2021, incident, the high-pressure alarm and trip activated after Kettle 3's pressure began to rise, and the rupture disk did not burst
At this peak pressure, Kettle 3's pressure was above the rupture disk's marked pressure setting but still within its burst tolerance range, and it did not burst to relieve Kettle 3's pressure

The tag on Kettle 3's recovered rupture disk post-incident indicated a marked burst pressure of 16.5 psig. According to purchase records, Yenkin-Majestic had been ordering rupture disks whose marked burst pressure fell within a manufacturing design range of 15.3-17 psig for Kettle 3, which did not match Kettle 3's documented process safety information

controls.name: Flammable gas detection system with email notification
controls.barrier_role: Detect flammable vapor release and notify personnel

supporting_text: Multiple flammable gas detectors inside the furnace room on the first floor started detecting an increasing concentration of flammable vapors during the release. The flammable gas detectors triggered automatic furnace shutdowns; however, they were not configured to sound an audible alarm

Yenkin-Majestic did not effectively utilize flammable gas detection systems and associated alarms to audibly notify onsite personnel of a hazardous gas release and the need to evacuate. During the incident, some of the gas monitors installed within the Yenkin-Majestic facility detected the flammable solvent release approximately one minute after the release began and sent an email to an offsite employee, but the monitors did not sound audible alarms to warn employees of the hazard

controls.name: Fire alarm system activation for evacuation
controls.barrier_role: Alert all personnel to evacuate facility in emergency

supporting_text: According to witness accounts, no one used the resin plant's intercom communication system to call for an evacuation or otherwise warn workers of the imminent danger. Post-incident, Yenkin-Majestic also confirmed that the fire alarm system had not been activated to warn workers to evacuate prior to the explosion
Employees working at or near the resin plant that night told CSB investigators that they did not hear any alarms or other warnings before the explosion

controls.name: Employee training on flammable vapor hazards and response
controls.barrier_role: Ensure employees recognize hazards and evacuate promptly
controls.lod_basis: Hazard awareness and emergency response training

supporting_text: Yenkin-Majestic did not specifically train its employees to recognize and respond to the presence of a flammable solvent vapor cloud and its associated hazards. During the incident, this overall lack of hazard recognition led to some personnel approaching the hazardous gas to investigate the release instead of initiating a plant-wide evacuation
At 12:03:30 a.m., an employee saw the white vapor from the outside of the building and began running into the resin plant to investigate what he thought was a steam leak

controls.name: Flame-resistant personal protective equipment (PPE)
controls.barrier_role: Protect employees from thermal burns during fire

supporting_text: Yenkin-Majestic allowed resin plant operators to wear cotton short-sleeved shirts while working in proximity to flammable materials unless they were performing specific tasks. Had the resin plant employees been required to wear flame-resistant personal protective equipment (PPE), multiple employee burn injuries resulting from the explosion and fire may have been reduced or prevented
Four of the injured employees were burned by the fire, two of whom received third-degree burns

controls.name: Process Hazard Analysis (PHA) and risk assessment
controls.barrier_role: Identify hazards and implement appropriate controls using hierarchy of controls
controls.lod_basis: Process safety management and design review

supporting_text: The hierarchy of controls is a risk management principle based on ranking hazard controls from most effective to least effective. On the day of the incident, the system design allowed an operator to add solvent to the hot-resin-filled kettle while, unknown to the operator, the agitator (which was supposed to mix the liquids in the kettle) was off

Yenkin-Majestic had not installed or configured interlocks to prevent solvent addition to the kettle when the agitator was off during normal batch operations and relied upon computer panel status indicators, that were not equipped with alarms, to communicate the agitator status to the operator

Although administrative controls are essential for reducing risk, they depend on human actions, perception, and judgment. Administrative controls should not be relied on alone, without additional robust design and engineering controls in highly hazardous chemical processes

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.
